modelTitle: Language as Mental Model & Reverse Compression
modelDescription: How language shapes thought and reality construction by serving as a compression algorithm for mental models

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: Language compresses a chair's infinite complexity (atoms, history, possibility) into a single word. The word points to the thing, but is not the thing.

book.title: Retool CEO David Hsu on Finding Product-Market Fit via Sales
book.author: ACQ2: The Acquired Interviews

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: Every time you speak, you lose information. Bandwidth is finite, so language forces you to choose which details matter and which to discard.
The Morning Star and Evening Star both refer to Venus, but they mean different things. Reference is what the word points to; meaning is how it gets there.
Most philosophical confusion comes from using words carelessly, not from actual metaphysical puzzles. Clarifying how we use language often dissolves the problem.
The words you choose bound what you can think. System dynamics looks invisible until you have language to see it; then it becomes obvious.
Chomsky showed grammar is not habit but structure. Children learn language so fast because they're building a complex system, not copying sounds.

book.title: A 'Razor' Is a Rule of Thumb That Simplifies Decision-Making
book.author: everhusk

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: Grice's razor: don't say more than you need to, don't say what you don't believe, make your meaning clear. Violate this and you create noise, not communication.
When two minds share mental models and language, they can think together at a level neither could alone. Symbols are the bridge that makes this possible.
Reading forces you to wrestle with someone else's words and build your own meaning from them. This effort is what makes reading transform how you think.
Humans alone use symbols to represent things not present. This one leap from gesture to symbol opened the door to all complexity and culture.

modelDescription: The distinction between word definitions and experiential understanding - knowing definitions vs. true comprehension

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: You can know every property of a chair (four legs, seat, height) and still not understand chairs until you sit in one. Description and experience are different knowledge.
The Morning Star and Evening Star refer to the same planet but mean different things to observers in different times. Same object, different knowledge.
Proper names pick out the thing itself, but they don't tell you what the thing is like. A name is a label that lets you talk about something without understanding it.
Philosophy tangles itself with words. Untangle the language and the problem vanishes. Most confusion is semantic, not substantive.
Everyone thinks they understand 'good,' but your actual conception differs from mine. We have the word, not necessarily the shared understanding.
A marriage proposal is three words; a legal contract is thousands. Both transmit information, but meaning is not about length. Meaning resolves uncertainty.
Information reduces surprise. If you already knew something, being told it carries zero information. Meaning comes from what you didn't expect.
Lifting weights means growing muscle; lifting in the gym means paying attention to form. The word stays the same, the meaning shifts with context.
Your mind holds competing rules in hierarchy. Understanding emerges when you build a system of concepts, not when you memorize isolated definitions.
You don't receive meaning passively. You build it actively through stories and theories. The same facts mean different things under different frameworks.
